findings.description: Stored XSS: display_name and action are f-string-injected into HTML table cells with zero escaping, so a malicious username executes script in the admin's browser the moment the report renders.
The user-supplied title is dropped into the page via str.format() unescaped, giving a second unescaped HTML injection sink alongside the row data.
The docstring promises an inclusive [start, end] range yet every query uses 'created_at < ?', silently dropping all events on the end day — the comment documents the exact opposite of the code.
Comparing a possibly-timestamped created_at against a bare date string (end.isoformat()) makes the boundary semantics a lexicographic coin flip when created_at stores time-of-day, not just a date.
save_report writes to caller-supplied out_path with no validation, allowing path traversal or absolute-path arbitrary file overwrite.
save_report performs no IOError handling, so a full disk or permission error during write propagates as an unhandled exception with no graceful degradation.
sqlite3.connect is called with no timeout, so a locked database blocks indefinitely on the default 5s-then-error behavior without explicit control.
rows is built via repeated string concatenation in a loop instead of a join, degrading to quadratic behavior on large event sets.
Each public method opens and closes its own connection with no shared connection or pooling, an inefficiency-flavored style nit rather than a defect.
